skill.name: azure-identity-java
skill.description: Azure Identity Java SDK for authentication with Azure services. Use when implementing DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, service principal, or any Azure authentication pattern in Java applications.
skill.summary: Microsoft Azure Identity SDK for Java - authentication library for Azure services with DefaultAzureCredential, managed identity, and service principal support.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation file for Microsoft's Azure Identity Java SDK. All static analysis findings are false positives: backtick characters in placeholder values, Azure service URLs in code examples, standard Kubernetes secret paths, and documented environment variables are all legitimate usage patterns. No malicious intent detected.

risk_factor_evidence.confidence_reasoning: Backtick characters detected are placeholder syntax in Java code examples (<tenant-id>, <client-id>), not shell command execution
URLs are legitimate Azure service endpoints (blob.core.windows.net, vault.azure.net, login.microsoftonline.com) used as documentation examples
File path is standard Kubernetes workload identity secret path (/var/run/secrets/azure/tokens/), documented by Microsoft
Environment variables (AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, etc.) are standard Azure credential configuration, properly documented for developers

content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive documentation for Microsoft's Azure Identity Java SDK, enabling Claude Code to implement secure authentication patterns including managed identity, service principals, and DefaultAzureCredential for Azure services.
actual_capabilities: Implement DefaultAzureCredential for automatic authentication across development and production environments
Configure Managed Identity for Azure App Service, Functions, AKS, and VMs without storing secrets
Set up Service Principal authentication with client secrets or certificates
Use Azure CLI credential for local development with az login
Implement workload identity for Kubernetes (AKS) authentication
Configure token caching for improved performance and reduced auth calls
limitations: This is a documentation skill - it provides reference information but does not execute code
Does not include runtime credential validation or testing capabilities
Does not provide Azure resource provisioning or management features
Requires existing Azure subscription and proper role assignments

use_cases.title: Add Azure Authentication to Java App
use_cases.description: Implement secure Azure authentication in new or existing Java applications using DefaultAzureCredential for seamless dev-to-prod transitions.
use_cases.target_user: Java developers building applications that access Azure services

use_cases.title: Migrate to Managed Identity
use_cases.description: Replace hardcoded service principal credentials with Azure Managed Identity for improved security and automatic credential rotation.

use_cases.title: Configure AKS Workload Identity
use_cases.description: Set up workload identity authentication for Java applications running on Azure Kubernetes Service.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Azure Auth Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I authenticate a Java application with Azure services using DefaultAzureCredential? Show me the Maven dependency and basic code example.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting started with Azure authentication in Java

prompt_templates.title: Managed Identity Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I configure Managed Identity authentication for a Java app running on Azure App Service?
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up passwordless authentication for Azure-hosted apps

prompt_templates.title: Service Principal with Certificate
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to authenticate using a service principal with a certificate in Java using ClientCertificateCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Workload Identity for AKS
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I configure WorkloadIdentityCredential for a Java application running on Azure Kubernetes Service?

output_examples.input: How do I set up Azure authentication in my Java app?
output_examples.output: Use DefaultAzureCredential for the simplest setup. Add the Maven dependency: com.azure:azure-identity. Then create a credential instance and use it with any Azure SDK client. DefaultAzureCredential automatically tries Environment, Managed Identity, Azure CLI, and other methods.

output_examples.input: What's the best way to authenticate in production?
output_examples.output: Use Managed Identity in production - it requires no secrets, automatically rotates credentials, and is the recommended approach for Azure-hosted applications. DefaultAzureCredential automatically uses Managed Identity when deployed to Azure App Service, Functions, AKS, or VMs.

output_examples.input: How do I use environment variables for CI/CD?
output_examples.output: Set these environment variables: AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, and AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET (or AZURE_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE_PATH for certificate-based auth). EnvironmentCredential will automatically pick these up when you build() an EnvironmentCredential instance.

best_practices: Always prefer DefaultAzureCredential - it automatically selects the best authentication method for each environment
Use Managed Identity in production - eliminates secret management and provides automatic credential rotation
Implement proper error handling for CredentialUnavailableException to handle cases when no credential is available
Use token caching (enabled by default) to reduce authentication overhead and improve performance
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode client secrets in source code - use environment variables or Managed Identity instead
Avoid using InteractiveBrowserCredential in server-side applications or CI/CD environments
Do not grant overly broad permissions to service principals - follow least privilege principle
Avoid excluding all credential types in DefaultAzureCredential unless you have a specific reason

faq.question: What is DefaultAzureCredential?
faq.answer: DefaultAzureCredential is a chain of credentials that tries multiple authentication methods in order: Environment variables, Workload Identity, Managed Identity, Azure CLI, Azure PowerShell, and Azure Developer CLI. It automatically selects the first available method, making it ideal for applications that run in different environments.

faq.question: How do I switch from dev to production authentication?
faq.answer: Use DefaultAzureCredential in both environments. Locally it will use Azure CLI (run 'az login'), while in Azure it automatically uses Managed Identity. No code changes required.

faq.question: What is Managed Identity?
faq.answer: Managed Identity is an Azure feature that provides automatically managed identity for resources running in Azure. It eliminates the need to store credentials - Azure automatically creates and rotates the identity. Use it for App Service, Functions, AKS, VMs, and other Azure services.

faq.question: How do I debug authentication issues?
faq.answer: Enable account identifier logging with enableAccountIdentifierLogging() on the credential builder. You can also set AZURE_LOG_LEVEL=verbose environment variable. Check that your Azure role assignments include the required permissions for the resources you're accessing.

faq.question: Can I use this with sovereign clouds?
faq.answer: Yes, configure the authority host for sovereign clouds like Azure Government or Azure China using AzureAuthorityHosts.AZURE_GOVERNMENT or AzureAuthorityHosts.AZURE_CHINA when building credentials.

faq.question: What credentials should I use for CI/CD?
faq.answer: Use EnvironmentCredential with service principal credentials stored as secure environment variables. This is the recommended approach for pipelines. Ensure the service principal has only the permissions required for the pipeline operations.
